explanation:
**Nutrition and fitness** are based on physical principles (energy balance) and biological principles (macronutrients, micronutrients). This calculator uses formulas validated by the scientific community (ACSM, ISSN, NIH).

## How It's Calculated

What supplements to take when starting the gym.

This calculator applies the formula using the values you enter, checking typical ranges and alerting you to values that seem unreasonable.

## Standard Caloric Distribution

| Macronutrient | % of Calories | Calories per Gram |
|---|---|---|
| Protein | 15-35% | 4 kcal/g |
| Carbohydrates | 45-65% | 4 kcal/g |
| Fats | 20-35% | 9 kcal/g |
| Alcohol | — | 7 kcal/g |
| Fiber | Minimum 25-38g per day | — |

## When to Consult a Nutritionist

- If you have health conditions (diabetes, high blood pressure, kidney disease) that require a personalized diet.
- If you want to gain significant muscle or compete in sports.
- If you have disordered eating patterns (binge eating, extreme restriction).
- If you haven't made [progress](/progress-scholarship-amount-requirements-2026) toward your goal in more than 3 months.
- To plan a complete vegan or vegetarian diet.

## Final Notes

This calculation is for reference only. For critical decisions (financial, medical, legal), consult with a specialized professional. Values are updated for 2026 and reviewed regularly.

faq.q: What supplements should I take as a beginner to the gym?
faq.a: Start with the fundamentals: whey protein powder, creatine monohydrate, and a basic multivitamin. These have strong scientific support. Always prioritize diet and training first, then add supplements.
Yes, but results vary by individual. Creatine and whey protein have solid evidence. BCAAs and multivitamins have weaker evidence. Remember: diet and training trump supplements every time.
Yes, creatine is one of the most researched supplements and is safe for healthy individuals. Standard dose is 3-5g per day after a loading phase. Stay well-hydrated.

faq.q: Should I take whey protein as a beginner?
faq.a: Whey protein is convenient and effective for meeting daily protein goals, especially post-workout. Aim for 1.2-1.5g of protein per kilogram of body weight daily.

faq.q: What's the best supplement stack for muscle gain?
faq.a: Beginner stack: whey protein, creatine, and a multivitamin. Advanced: add beta-alanine and BCAAs if needed. Always prioritize consistent training and adequate calories.

faq.q: How much protein do I need per day?
faq.a: Sedentary: 0.8g per kilogram of body weight. Active: 1.2-1.5g/kg. Muscle building: 1.6-2.2g/kg of lean body mass. Minimal benefit exists beyond 2.2g/kg.

faq.q: When should I start taking supplements?
faq.a: After establishing consistent training (4+ weeks) and solid eating habits. Supplements work best as additions to, not replacements for, proper nutrition and training.

faq.q: Are BCAAs worth the cost for beginners?
faq.a: Probably not. Whey protein already contains all essential amino acids. BCAAs are most useful during fasted cardio or when calorie-restricted. Whole foods and whey are usually enough.
